description: >
  Builds the full run-of-show for a community AMA or live event - mod roles, question intake/filtering plan, and a minute-by-minute timeline. Triggers: "plan our AMA with the founder next week", "build a runsheet for the community event", /ama-event-runsheet.

  risk: low
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /ama-event-runsheet

Produces the operational runsheet for a live community AMA or event - who's doing what, how questions get filtered, and a minute-by-minute timeline - so the event doesn't rely on mods improvising roles in real time.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before any live community event with a guest, founder, or notable figure taking questions - especially first-time AMAs where the mod team hasn't run one before and risks a chaotic, unmoderated question flood.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Event format: text-based AMA (questions posted in a channel), voice/stage event, or a hybrid with pre-submitted plus live questions
- Guest's availability window (total time) and any topics they've asked to avoid
- Mod team size available to work the event, and existing tools (a bot for question queueing, thread/forum channel, reaction-based upvoting)
- Expected attendance scale - a 50-person community and a 5,000-person community need very different question-intake mechanics

## Workflow

1. Decide the question-intake mechanism before anything else: pre-submission window with mod curation (best for large/high-risk events), live free-for-all in a single channel (only works for small, low-risk communities), or a hybrid queue with a bot - mismatch here is the most common cause of AMA chaos.
2. Assign explicit mod roles for the live event: a question curator (selects/queues next question), a chat moderator (handles off-topic/spam during the event), and a timekeeper (keeps the guest on pace) - one person cannot do all three during a busy event.
3. Pre-screen submitted questions against the guest's stated off-limit topics and against basic quality (not a duplicate, not hostile bait) before the event starts, building a ranked queue rather than relying on live judgment calls under pressure.
4. Build a minute-by-minute timeline: welcome/intro, guest intro, question segment (broken into blocks if long), a buffer near the end for follow-ups, and a clear close - with a specific person responsible for keeping it on schedule.
5. Prepare 3-5 backup questions from the mod team in case live submissions run dry or skew too repetitive, so there's never a dead silence moment.
6. Write the exact channel-pinned instructions members will see for how to submit/ask questions during the event, matched to the chosen intake mechanism from step 1.
7. Plan the post-event wrap: who archives the Q&A into a readable recap, and whether/how it gets shared outside the live channel (show notes-style summary, pinned thread).

## Output format

```
AMA/EVENT RUNSHEET - [Event name] - [Date/time]

Question intake mechanism: [pre-submit / live / hybrid]
Guest off-limit topics: ...

MOD ROLES:
Question curator: [name]
Chat moderator: [name]
Timekeeper: [name]

TIMELINE:
[Time] - Welcome/intro
[Time] - Guest intro
[Time] - Question block 1
[Time] - Question block 2
[Time] - Buffer/follow-ups
[Time] - Close

QUESTION QUEUE (pre-screened, ranked):
1. ...
2. ...

BACKUP QUESTIONS (if submissions run dry):
- ...

MEMBER-FACING SUBMISSION INSTRUCTIONS (pinned):
"..."

POST-EVENT: Recap owner: [name] - Distribution: [where it gets shared]
```

## Do
- Assign the curator/chat-mod/timekeeper roles to specific named people, not "the mod team" generally
- Pre-screen questions against guest off-limits before the event, not live under pressure
- Prepare backup questions so there's never dead air

## Don't
- Don't run a large or high-risk AMA as pure live free-for-all with no pre-screening
- Don't let one mod try to curate, moderate, and timekeep simultaneously
- Don't skip the post-event recap plan - unarchived AMAs lose most of their long-term value

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Question intake mechanism matches the event's actual scale/risk level
- [ ] Mod roles are assigned individually, not left as a shared responsibility
- [ ] Post-event recap ownership and distribution plan is specified
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /ama-event-runsheet or describe the task from the skill description.

  Pulls real activity data from your community platform and produces a plain-English health report covering engagement trend, mod workload, and early warning signs. Triggers: "give me a health report on the community this month", "how is server engagement trending", /community-health-report.
metadata:

  cadence: monthly
  risk: low

  integrations: "@discord, @slack"

---
# /community-health-report

Pulls actual member/message/moderation activity from Discord or Slack and turns it into a plain-English monthly health report - real trend direction and early warning signs, not a vague "engagement is good" summary.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

At a regular cadence (monthly is typical) or after a member-facing change (new rule, new channel structure, big member influx) when the team wants a real read on whether the community is actually healthier or just busier.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Which platform holds the data (Discord server, Slack workspace) and which channels/timeframe to pull
- The comparison baseline - last month, last quarter, or since a specific change was made
- Whether mod action logs (warnings, timeouts, bans) are accessible via the connector or need to be supplied manually
- What "healthy" means for this specific community (a support community cares about response time; a hobbyist community cares about original posts vs. reposts) - this shapes which metrics matter most

## Connector access (required)

This skill cannot run from text alone - it must call live integrations.
1. Check your AI tool's connector/integration settings (for example: Grok's `/mcps` command, Cursor's MCP settings, Claude's Integrations panel, or your tool's equivalent) and confirm Discord and/or Slack are enabled and authenticated. The exact steps vary by tool - if you're not sure how, just ask your AI assistant directly.
2. Use `search_tool` to discover the available tools for each connector in your current session.
3. Call tools with `use_tool` using the exact tool name your session exposes.
4. Never claim to have accessed Discord or Slack without a successful tool call - if the connector isn't available, stop and tell the user which app/integration to enable, and offer this skill's text-only fallback (report can still be built if the user pastes exported stats manually).

### Required integrations
- **@discord** - Discord
  - Example tools you might see: list_channels, get_messages, get_member_count, get_audit_log
- **@slack** - Slack
  - Example tools you might see: conversations_history, users_list, list_channels

## Workflow

1. Pull raw activity data via the connector: member count/growth, message volume by channel over the comparison window, and mod action counts (warnings/timeouts/bans) if the audit log is accessible.
2. Compare this period against the stated baseline, not against an assumed "normal" - express changes as percentages, and flag if the comparison window is too short to be meaningful.
3. Break down engagement by channel, not just server-wide totals - a server-wide increase can mask one channel dying while another spikes, which changes what action (if any) is needed.
4. Identify the ratio of unique active posters to total members - rising member count with flat or shrinking unique posters is an early warning sign of a community becoming a lurker crowd, even if it looks fine on the surface.
5. Cross-reference mod action volume against member/message growth - a disproportionate rise in warnings/bans relative to growth signals a culture or moderation problem worth flagging even if overall activity looks healthy.
6. Note any channel or time-of-day pattern in moderation actions (e.g., most incidents cluster in one channel or around a specific recurring event) since this points to a fixable structural cause, not just "people are being bad."
7. Translate the data into 2-3 concrete recommended actions (not just observations) - e.g., "unique poster ratio dropped 15%, consider a low-effort engagement prompt in the quiet channels" rather than ending on raw numbers alone.

## Output format

```
COMMUNITY HEALTH REPORT - [Community name] - Period: [dates] vs. [baseline period]

HEADLINE METRICS:
Member count: [current] ([+/-X%] vs. baseline)
Total messages: [current] ([+/-X%] vs. baseline)
Unique active posters: [current] ([+/-X%] vs. baseline)
Unique-poster-to-member ratio: [current] vs. [baseline] - trend: [up/down/flat]

BY-CHANNEL BREAKDOWN:
[Channel] - messages: [N] ([+/-X%]) - notable: ...

MODERATION ACTIVITY:
Warnings/timeouts/bans this period: [N] vs. [baseline]
Pattern noted: [channel/time clustering, or "none evident"]

EARLY WARNING SIGNS:
- ...

RECOMMENDED ACTIONS:
1. ...
2. ...

DATA CONFIDENCE NOTE: [full connector data / partial - some figures manually supplied]
```

## Do
- Pull real numbers via the connector before writing any conclusion - never estimate engagement figures
- Track the unique-poster ratio, not just raw message/member counts - it's the more honest health signal
- Flag when the comparison window is too short for the trend to be meaningful

## Don't
- Don't claim to have pulled Discord/Slack data without an actual successful tool call
- Don't present a healthy-looking total message count without checking whether it's concentrated in one channel or spread across a shrinking active base
- Don't end the report on raw numbers without translating them into specific recommended actions

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results
- [ ] Unique-poster ratio is calculated and compared, not just raw totals
- [ ] Report ends with specific recommended actions, not just observations
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /community-health-report or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: escalation-decision-tree
description: >
  Builds a branching decision tree mods can follow in the moment to decide whether to warn, escalate, or bring in an admin/legal for a specific incident type. Triggers: "what's our escalation process for harassment reports", "build a decision tree for when mods should escalate", /escalation-decision-tree.
metadata:
  category: community-moderation
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /escalation-decision-tree

Produces a branching if-this-then-that decision tree for a specific incident category, so a mod handling a live report in the moment has a clear next action instead of guessing or waiting for an admin to wake up.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When a mod team has had incidents handled inconsistently (different mods making different calls on similar reports) or is onboarding new mods who don't yet have the judgment built up from experience - most useful for the high-stakes categories (harassment, doxxing, self-harm mentions, brigading) where hesitation costs real harm.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific incident category this tree covers (harassment report, doxxing/personal info leak, suicide/self-harm mention, coordinated raid/brigading, minor-safety concern) - build one tree per category, not one tree trying to cover everything
- Current mod team structure: are there tiers (trial mod, mod, admin/owner) with different permissions, and what's each tier actually allowed to action alone
- Existing tools available (timeout/mute, ban, message delete, platform trust & safety reporting) and any the team lacks
- Whether any category here has legal/duty-of-care implications (self-harm, minors, threats) requiring a specific off-platform response (crisis resources, law enforcement contact) already decided by the community owner

## Safety

- Propose before acting: this document should be reviewed and approved by the community owner/admin before mods rely on it live, especially the self-harm/minor-safety/legal-threat branches.
- For self-harm, threats of violence, or minor-safety content: the tree must route to human admin judgment and appropriate off-platform resources immediately - never let an AI-authored tree be the sole gate on a life-safety decision.

## Workflow

1. Start the tree with the fastest, most objective branch point: is this a clear-cut violation (rule text obviously matches) or ambiguous/needs-context? Objective cases get a fast-track action; ambiguous cases route to a "gather more context" branch rather than a snap call.
2. For clear-cut cases, map directly to the tiered consequence (reference the community's existing consequence ladder) and specify exactly what a single mod can action alone versus what needs a second mod's sign-off.
3. For ambiguous cases, give the mod 2-3 concrete context-gathering questions to answer before deciding (e.g., "is this the user's first incident," "is there a pattern across channels," "did the target request action") rather than leaving "use judgment" as the only guidance.
4. Build a hard branch for anything touching safety-critical categories (self-harm, minors, credible threats, doxxing) that bypasses normal mod judgment entirely and routes straight to admin/owner plus any predetermined off-platform resource or reporting step.
5. Specify exactly who gets pinged/notified at each escalation point (which role, which channel, response time expectation) - a tree that says "escalate to admin" without saying how is not actionable at 2am.
6. Add a documentation branch at the end of every path - what gets logged, where, regardless of which branch was taken, so patterns across incidents are visible later.
7. Include one worked example per major branch showing a realistic incident walked through the tree start to finish, so new mods can pattern-match against it.

## Output format

```
ESCALATION TREE - [Incident category]

START: Is this clear-cut (obvious rule match) or ambiguous?

CLEAR-CUT →
  Action: [specific consequence tier]
  Mod alone or second sign-off required: ...
  
AMBIGUOUS →
  Gather: [question 1] / [question 2] / [question 3]
  Then → [route to clear-cut branch above, or escalate]

SAFETY-CRITICAL (self-harm / minors / credible threats / doxxing) →
  BYPASS normal judgment. Immediate action: ...
  Notify: [specific role/person] via [specific channel]
  Off-platform resource/step: ...

ESCALATION CONTACTS:
[Role] - notify via [channel] - expected response time: ...

DOCUMENTATION (all branches):
Log: [what fields] in [where]

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Incident: ... → Branch taken: ... → Action: ... → Logged as: ...
```

## Do
- Build one tree per incident category rather than one tree trying to cover everything
- Name the specific role, channel, and response-time expectation at every escalation point
- Route safety-critical categories to a hard bypass, never normal mod judgment alone

## Don't
- Don't leave "use your judgment" as the only guidance for ambiguous cases - give concrete questions to resolve ambiguity first
- Don't let this tree be the sole authority on self-harm, minor-safety, or credible-threat situations without human admin review built in
- Don't publish without owner/admin sign-off, especially on safety-critical branches

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Approval gate present before mods rely on this live, especially safety-critical branches
- [ ] Every escalation point names a specific role/channel/response time, not "escalate to admin"
- [ ] Safety-critical categories bypass normal judgment and route to human admin plus real resources
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /escalation-decision-tree or describe the task from the skill description.

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When a mod team has had incidents handled inconsistently (different mods making different calls on similar reports) or is onboarding new mods who don't yet have the judgment built up from experience - most useful for the high-stakes categories (harassment, doxxing, self-harm mentions, brigading) where hesitation costs real harm.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific incident category this tree covers (harassment report, doxxing/personal info leak, suicide/self-harm mention, coordinated raid/brigading, minor-safety concern) - build one tree per category, not one tree trying to cover everything
- Current mod team structure: are there tiers (trial mod, mod, admin/owner) with different permissions, and what's each tier actually allowed to action alone
- Existing tools available (timeout/mute, ban, message delete, platform trust & safety reporting) and any the team lacks
- Whether any category here has legal/duty-of-care implications (self-harm, minors, threats) requiring a specific off-platform response (crisis resources, law enforcement contact) already decided by the community owner

## Safety

- Propose before acting: this document should be reviewed and approved by the community owner/admin before mods rely on it live, especially the self-harm/minor-safety/legal-threat branches.
- For self-harm, threats of violence, or minor-safety content: the tree must route to human admin judgment and appropriate off-platform resources immediately - never let an AI-authored tree be the sole gate on a life-safety decision.

## Workflow

1. Start the tree with the fastest, most objective branch point: is this a clear-cut violation (rule text obviously matches) or ambiguous/needs-context? Objective cases get a fast-track action; ambiguous cases route to a "gather more context" branch rather than a snap call.
2. For clear-cut cases, map directly to the tiered consequence (reference the community's existing consequence ladder) and specify exactly what a single mod can action alone versus what needs a second mod's sign-off.
3. For ambiguous cases, give the mod 2-3 concrete context-gathering questions to answer before deciding (e.g., "is this the user's first incident," "is there a pattern across channels," "did the target request action") rather than leaving "use judgment" as the only guidance.
4. Build a hard branch for anything touching safety-critical categories (self-harm, minors, credible threats, doxxing) that bypasses normal mod judgment entirely and routes straight to admin/owner plus any predetermined off-platform resource or reporting step.
5. Specify exactly who gets pinged/notified at each escalation point (which role, which channel, response time expectation) - a tree that says "escalate to admin" without saying how is not actionable at 2am.
6. Add a documentation branch at the end of every path - what gets logged, where, regardless of which branch was taken, so patterns across incidents are visible later.
7. Include one worked example per major branch showing a realistic incident walked through the tree start to finish, so new mods can pattern-match against it.

## Output format

```
ESCALATION TREE - [Incident category]

START: Is this clear-cut (obvious rule match) or ambiguous?

CLEAR-CUT →
  Action: [specific consequence tier]
  Mod alone or second sign-off required: ...
  
AMBIGUOUS →
  Gather: [question 1] / [question 2] / [question 3]
  Then → [route to clear-cut branch above, or escalate]

SAFETY-CRITICAL (self-harm / minors / credible threats / doxxing) →
  BYPASS normal judgment. Immediate action: ...
  Notify: [specific role/person] via [specific channel]
  Off-platform resource/step: ...

ESCALATION CONTACTS:
[Role] - notify via [channel] - expected response time: ...

DOCUMENTATION (all branches):
Log: [what fields] in [where]

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Incident: ... → Branch taken: ... → Action: ... → Logged as: ...
```

## Do
- Build one tree per incident category rather than one tree trying to cover everything
- Name the specific role, channel, and response-time expectation at every escalation point
- Route safety-critical categories to a hard bypass, never normal mod judgment alone

## Don't
- Don't leave "use your judgment" as the only guidance for ambiguous cases - give concrete questions to resolve ambiguity first
- Don't let this tree be the sole authority on self-harm, minor-safety, or credible-threat situations without human admin review built in
- Don't publish without owner/admin sign-off, especially on safety-critical branches

- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Approval gate present before mods rely on this live, especially safety-critical branches
- [ ] Every escalation point names a specific role/channel/response time, not "escalate to admin"
- [ ] Safety-critical categories bypass normal judgment and route to human admin plus real resources

# /mod-team-onboarding-guide

Produces the onboarding packet and first-30-days plan for a new moderator - tool access, judgment calibration through real examples, and a shadow period - designed so a new mod isn't making live enforcement calls on day one with no calibration.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Whenever a community promotes or hires a new moderator - especially communities that have been onboarding mods informally ("here's the rules doc, good luck") and have seen inconsistent enforcement or a new mod burning out from lack of support in their first weeks.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Existing moderation policy/consequence ladder and tools (bots, permission tiers) the new mod needs access to
- Team structure - who is this mod's point of contact/mentor for their first weeks, and what does the escalation chain look like
- Any past incidents or edge cases worth using as calibration examples (anonymized if needed) - real past cases teach judgment far better than abstract rules
- New mod's availability/timezone relative to the community's peak activity hours, since shadow scheduling depends on this

## Workflow

1. Separate the packet into access/tools setup (mechanical, do first) from judgment calibration (the harder part) - a new mod who has bot permissions but no calibration is more dangerous than one who's slower to get access but understands the community's actual norms.
2. Build a first-week shadow period where the new mod observes and proposes actions on real incidents without actually executing them, with a mentor confirming or correcting each call - this is where most of the real training happens, not from reading the policy doc alone.
3. Curate 4-6 real (anonymized if needed) past incidents spanning easy/clear-cut to genuinely ambiguous, and have the new mod work through what they'd do - then compare against what was actually decided, discussing any mismatch. This calibrates judgment far faster than rules alone.
4. Assign a specific named point of contact for the new mod's first 30 days for "is this the right call" questions - an onboarding plan with no named support contact tends to leave new mods either paralyzed or overconfident.
5. Set explicit boundaries for the first weeks: which actions the new mod can take solo (e.g., clear-cut warnings) versus which require a second mod's confirmation before executing (e.g., anything ban-tier or ambiguous) - loosen this over a defined timeline, not indefinitely.
6. Include the "soft skills" that don't fit in a rules doc - how to de-escalate without matching energy, when to loop in the team versus handle solo, how to document decisions - since these are usually learned by osmosis and rarely written down.
7. Schedule a real 30-day check-in with a two-way conversation (how's it going for the mod, and feedback from the team on calls they've made) rather than assuming onboarding is done once access is granted.

## Output format

```
MOD ONBOARDING GUIDE - [Community name] - New mod: [name]

WEEK 1 - ACCESS & SHADOW:
Tools/access granted: ...
Point of contact: [name]
Shadow period: [duration] - observes and proposes, mentor confirms

CALIBRATION EXERCISES (past incidents, work through then compare):
1. [Anonymized scenario] - actual decision was: ...
2. ...

SOLO VS. CONFIRM-FIRST BOUNDARIES (first [N] weeks):
Can action alone: ...
Needs second mod confirmation: ...
Loosens to full autonomy on: [date/milestone]

SOFT-SKILLS NOTES:
De-escalation approach: ...
When to loop in the team: ...
Documentation habits: ...

30-DAY CHECK-IN:
Scheduled for: [date]
Covers: new mod's experience + team feedback on calls made
```

## Do
- Use real (anonymized) past incidents for calibration rather than abstract rule explanations alone
- Name a specific point-of-contact person, not "the team" generally
- Set a defined timeline for when solo-action boundaries loosen

## Don't
- Don't grant full moderation permissions with no shadow period or calibration exercises first
- Don't skip the 30-day check-in - assume onboarding is "done" the moment access is granted
- Don't leave solo-vs-confirm-first boundaries vague - new mods need explicit lines, not implied ones

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Calibration exercises use real (anonymized) incidents, not hypotheticals only
- [ ] A named point of contact is specified, not a vague team reference
- [ ] A real 30-day check-in is scheduled, not left implicit
```

# /moderation-policy-doc

Produces a moderation policy specific to this community's actual culture, size, and risk areas - rules, tiered consequences, and enforcement notes mod teams can actually apply consistently, not a copy of a generic ToS.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When starting a new community, formalizing rules that have only existed as mod team folklore, or after an incident revealed the current rules didn't cover the situation clearly enough.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Community topic/purpose, approximate size, and age range if relevant (a gaming server for adults needs different rules than a fan community with minors present)
- Specific problems this community has actually had (spam type, harassment patterns, off-topic conflict, self-promo abuse) - rules should target real recurring issues, not a generic list
- Current mod team size and tooling (bots for auto-mod, manual review only) since enforcement mechanisms depend on capacity
- Platform (Discord, forum, Slack) since enforcement tools differ

## Safety

- Propose before acting: present the draft policy for review; wait for explicit confirmation before publishing it as the community's binding rules.
- Flag any rule area touching legal exposure (harassment, doxxing, content involving minors, illegal content) as requiring the owner's own legal judgment - don't present AI-drafted legal thresholds as authoritative.

## Workflow

1. Group rules into clear categories (e.g., conduct/harassment, content standards, spam/self-promotion, platform-specific like voice channel etiquette) rather than one flat numbered list - this is what makes enforcement lookups fast for mods mid-incident.
2. Write each rule as a specific, testable standard ("no unsolicited DMs advertising products/services" beats "be respectful"), grounded in the actual problems the user described, not generic community-guidelines boilerplate.
3. Build a tiered consequence ladder (e.g., warning → timeout → temp ban → permanent ban) and map violation types to entry points on that ladder - a first-time minor spam violation and a targeted harassment case shouldn't start at the same rung.
4. Add an explicit "zero-tolerance, skip the ladder" list for severe violations (doxxing, threats, illegal content, hate speech) so mods know these bypass graduated warnings entirely.
5. Include a short section on mod discretion and documentation - mods should log the reasoning for enforcement actions above a warning, protecting both the community and the mod from disputes later.
6. Address at least one ambiguous real-world edge case explicitly (e.g., "jokes that reference the rules ironically," "arguments that started respectful and escalated") since these are where policies usually fail in practice.
7. Write a short public-facing summary version (the actual #rules channel text) separate from the longer internal mod reference - members read the short version; mods need the detailed one.

## Output format

```
MODERATION POLICY - [Community name]

PUBLIC RULES (short, for #rules channel):
1. ...
2. ...

INTERNAL MOD REFERENCE:

Category: Conduct & Harassment
- Rule: ... | Consequence ladder: ... | Zero-tolerance? [y/n]

Category: Content Standards
- ...

Category: Spam & Self-Promotion
- ...

Category: [Platform-specific, e.g. Voice/Channel Etiquette]
- ...

ZERO-TOLERANCE VIOLATIONS (skip ladder, immediate escalation):
- ...

DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENT: 
Mods must log [what] for any action above [tier].

EDGE CASE GUIDANCE:
- Scenario: ... → Guidance: ...
```

## Do
- Ground every rule in a real problem this specific community has faced or is likely to face
- Separate the short public rules from the detailed internal mod reference
- Give mods an explicit zero-tolerance bypass list for severe cases

## Don't
- Don't publish generic "be kind" rules with no specific, testable standard behind them
- Don't present this as final/binding without explicit user sign-off, especially on legal-adjacent rule areas
- Don't skip the edge-case section - that's where real disputes happen

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Approval gate present before treating the policy as published/binding
- [ ] Rules are specific and testable, not vague platitudes
- [ ] At least one real edge case is addressed explicitly
```

# /rule-violation-appeal-process

Designs both halves of an appeal system - the member-facing submission process and the mod-side review template - so contested moderation decisions get a fair, consistent second look instead of being handled ad hoc in DMs.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When a community has no formal way for members to contest a ban/timeout beyond DMing a mod directly (which tends to go to whichever mod is most sympathetic or most annoyed), or after a disputed enforcement decision revealed the community has no consistent review process.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Current enforcement/consequence ladder (so the appeal process can reference real tiers - a warning appeal looks different from a permanent ban appeal)
- Team size and structure - is there a distinct "review" tier (e.g., senior mods or owner) separate from whoever issued the original action, since appeals reviewed by the same person who banned someone aren't credible
- Platform/mechanism available for submissions (a form, a specific ticket channel, email) 
- Whether any violation categories should be explicitly non-appealable (e.g., illegal content, confirmed doxxing) versus everything else being eligible

## Safety

- Propose before acting: present this process for owner/admin approval before publishing it as policy - appeal outcomes affect real people's community access and should reflect the owner's actual risk tolerance.
- Flag that the reviewer must not be the same mod who issued the original action, to preserve basic fairness and avoid the appearance of a rubber-stamp process.

## Workflow

1. Define which violation tiers are appealable at all - most communities should allow appeals for warnings/timeouts/temp bans, and it's reasonable to make certain severe categories (confirmed doxxing, threats, illegal content) explicitly non-appealable; state this distinction clearly so members aren't submitting appeals doomed from the start.
2. Design the submission mechanism to require specific information (what action they're appealing, when, their account/username, their explanation) rather than an open-ended "why should we unban you" that produces unusable responses.
3. Set a clear response-time expectation (e.g., "appeals reviewed within 5 business days") and state it publicly - an appeal process with no timeline erodes trust as much as no process at all.
4. Require the reviewer to be someone other than the mod who issued the original action - build this into the process explicitly, not as an informal norm that gets skipped under staffing pressure.
5. Build the mod-side review template to force three things: what the original violation/evidence was, what the appellant said, and what changed the reviewer's assessment (if anything) - a decision with no stated reasoning is not a real review, just a second coin flip.
6. Define possible outcomes beyond binary uphold/overturn - e.g., reduce a permanent ban to a time-boxed one, uphold with a probationary re-entry condition - so the process isn't forced into all-or-nothing outcomes that don't fit nuanced cases.

# /toxic-language-response-templates

Produces a set of scenario-specific mod response scripts for de-escalating toxic behavior in the moment - worded to de-escalate and assert authority calmly, not to shame, argue, or match the user's energy.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When a mod team keeps improvising responses to recurring toxic scenarios in the moment (often inconsistently, sometimes escalating conflict further) and wants pre-written, tested language ready to paste during an actual incident.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Which specific scenarios need templates (e.g., two members escalating an argument, a pile-on/dogpiling situation, someone using a slur or edge-of-the-line language, a member being aggressive toward a mod's decision, someone baiting/bad-faith arguing)
- Community tone (a template that fits a formal professional Discord will feel out of place in a casual gaming server, and vice versa)
- Whether the mod should act publicly in-channel, via DM, or both for each scenario
- The community's actual consequence ladder (so templates can reference real next steps, e.g., "this is your first warning" vs. "this violates rule 4 and results in a timeout")

## Safety

- Propose before acting: these are templates for the mod to use, not for autonomous posting - a human mod reads context and picks/adapts the right template before sending.
- Flag scenarios involving slurs, hate speech, or targeted harassment as needing the zero-tolerance policy path, not just a de-escalation message - the template addresses the moment, but the incident still needs to be logged and actioned per policy.

## Workflow

1. For each scenario, write the template to do exactly three things: name the specific behavior neutrally (not the person's character), state the boundary/rule being enforced, and give a clear next step (stop now / take it to DMs / this is a warning) - skip generic "let's be nice" language that has no enforcement teeth.
2. Keep the tone flat and calm, never sarcastic, mocking, or matching the aggressor's energy - a mod response that sounds annoyed or superior tends to escalate rather than de-escalate, even when the mod is right.
3. For dogpiling/pile-on scenarios, address the group dynamic explicitly ("this thread has turned into several people against one, pausing it here") rather than singling out only the last person who posted, since pile-ons are a collective pattern.
4. For borderline language (not a clear-cut slur but close to the line), write a template that names the specific words/phrases and states the standard going forward, rather than an ambiguous "watch your language" that doesn't teach where the line is.
5. For a member arguing with a mod's decision, write a template that acknowledges their right to disagree while stating clearly that the channel/thread is not the venue for re-litigating it, with a specific alternative (appeal process, DM the mod team) - don't just assert authority with no outlet for disagreement.
6. Include a short public-channel version and a longer DM version for scenarios where a private follow-up is more appropriate than public correction, since some situations shouldn't be handled in front of an audience.
7. Note for each template which ones must be paired with an actual logged action (timeout, warning recorded) versus which are pure de-escalation with no formal consequence attached.

## Output format

```
TOXIC-LANGUAGE RESPONSE TEMPLATES

SCENARIO: [e.g., two members escalating an argument]
Public channel version: "..."
DM version (if applicable): "..."
Pair with logged action? [yes/no - which tier]

SCENARIO: [e.g., dogpiling/pile-on]
...

SCENARIO: [e.g., borderline/edge-of-line language]
...

SCENARIO: [e.g., member disputing a mod decision publicly]
...
```

## Do
- Keep every template flat, calm, and non-mocking regardless of how the other party is behaving
- Name specific behavior and rules, never character judgments about the person
- Give an explicit next step or outlet (DM, appeal process) rather than just shutting a conversation down

## Don't
- Don't write templates that sound sarcastic or superior - this reliably escalates rather than de-escalates
- Don't treat slur/hate-speech scenarios as pure de-escalation moments - they still require logging and the zero-tolerance policy path
- Don't single out only the most recent poster in a group pile-on dynamic

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Approval gate present - these are mod-selected templates, not auto-sent messages
- [ ] Every template names behavior/rule specifically, never character attacks
- [ ] Zero-tolerance scenarios are flagged as needing logged action, not just a de-escalation line
```

# /weekly-community-digest

Turns a week of scattered community activity into a single scannable digest post highlighting what actually mattered - not a mechanical channel-by-channel activity log nobody reads to the end.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

At the end of each week (or whatever cadence the community uses) when there's real activity to round up - new member wins, standout discussions, upcoming events - and the goal is a member-facing recap post, not an internal report.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Raw material for the week: notable threads/discussions, any member achievements or shoutout-worthy contributions, upcoming events/deadlines, and any announcements already made
- Where this digest gets posted (a dedicated channel, a newsletter, both) since length/formatting expectations differ
- Whether member shoutouts require their permission before naming them publicly (a good default: ask first for anything beyond a simple "great post" credit)
- Community tone, so the digest voice matches (casual/fun vs. professional recap)

## Workflow

1. Scan the week's raw material and select only what's genuinely digest-worthy - a handful of standout threads/moments, not an exhaustive log of every channel's activity. If nothing much happened in a channel, it's fine to leave it out entirely rather than padding.
2. Lead with the single most interesting or highest-engagement item of the week, not a chronological recap - members skim digests, so the best material needs to be visible immediately.
3. Give real member shoutouts specific credit for what they actually did (helped someone solve a problem, shared something valuable, hit a milestone) rather than generic "shoutout to active members" - specificity is what makes shoutouts feel earned rather than filler.
4. Summarize standout discussion threads in 1-2 sentences with a link back to the thread, capturing the interesting tension or takeaway, not just the topic name.
5. List upcoming events/deadlines clearly and near the top or bottom (pick one consistent position each week) so members can find them without reading the whole digest.
6. Keep total length scannable in under a minute - cut ruthlessly; a digest that takes five minutes to read stops getting read after a few weeks.
7. Close with one light, community-voice sign-off or a forward-looking teaser (what's coming next week) to keep momentum rather than ending abruptly after the last bullet.

## Output format

```
WEEKLY DIGEST - [Community name] - Week of [date]

TOP OF THE WEEK: [single best item, 1-2 sentences, link if applicable]

STANDOUT DISCUSSIONS:
- [Thread topic] - [1-2 sentence takeaway] - [link]
- ...

MEMBER SHOUTOUTS:
- [Name] - [specific thing they did]
- ...

UPCOMING:
- [Event/deadline] - [date]
- ...

[Sign-off / teaser for next week]
```

## Do
- Select only genuinely notable material - omitting a quiet channel entirely is better than padding
- Give shoutouts with specific detail about what the person actually did
- Lead with the single best item, not chronological order

## Don't
- Don't produce an exhaustive channel-by-channel activity log - that's a report, not a digest
- Don't name members in shoutouts beyond simple credit without checking if anything more personal needs their permission first
- Don't let the digest run long enough that members stop reading it after the first few weeks

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Content is curated/selective, not an exhaustive activity log
- [ ] Member shoutouts include specific, earned detail, not generic praise
- [ ] Total length is scannable in under a minute
```

# /welcome-flow-copy

Produces the full new-member welcome sequence - instant greet, rules/verification prompt, and a follow-up nudge - written to convert a silent new joiner into someone who posts within their first week.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When launching a new community, or when an existing community notices high join numbers but low actual participation - the welcome flow is often the highest-leverage fix for a "ghost town" feeling despite decent member count.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Community topic/purpose and personality (formal/professional vs. casual/meme-heavy - the welcome tone needs to match or new members feel like they joined the wrong place)
- Verification/rules-acceptance mechanism in place (reaction role, form, bot gate) since the copy needs to reference the actual mechanic
- What "engaged" looks like for this community - introducing themselves in a channel, picking roles, posting in a specific starter channel - pick one concrete first action to funnel toward
- Whether there's an existing automated welcome bot message to build on/replace, or this is being built from scratch

## Workflow

1. Write the instant auto-greet to do three things only: welcome them by name, tell them the one specific next action to take (not a list of five links), and set tone in the first line so the community's personality is immediately legible.
2. Keep the rules-acknowledgment prompt short and specific - reference the actual mechanism (react to confirm, type a command, fill a form) rather than generic "please read the rules."
3. Funnel toward exactly one concrete first action (e.g., "introduce yourself in #introductions" or "grab a role in #roles") rather than presenting the whole server map at once - new members overwhelmed by options tend to do nothing.
4. Design a short first-week follow-up nudge (automated DM or a scheduled check by a mod) for members who joined but never posted, distinct from the instant welcome - silent joiners are the biggest churn risk and rarely get a second touchpoint.
5. Include a lightweight "who's here" prompt in the introduction template (2-3 fill-in-the-blank fields max) so introducing yourself doesn't feel like a job application.
6. Match reading level and tone exactly to the community's actual culture - a Gen-Z gaming Discord and a professional B2B community need entirely different voices, and mismatched tone reads as inauthentic immediately.
7. Add a note for mods on what "successful onboarding" looks like operationally (e.g., % of joiners who complete verification within 24 hours) so the flow's effectiveness can be tracked, not just assumed.

1. INSTANT AUTO-GREET (on join):
"..."

2. RULES/VERIFICATION PROMPT:
"..."

3. INTRODUCTION TEMPLATE (posted in #introductions or similar):

Fill-in fields: ...

4. FIRST-WEEK NUDGE (for members who joined but haven't posted):
"..."
Trigger timing: [e.g., 3-4 days post-join, no activity]

Mod tracking note: [what metric signals onboarding is working]
```

# /welcome-flow-copy

Produces the full new-member welcome sequence - instant greet, rules/verification prompt, and a follow-up nudge - written to convert a silent new joiner into someone who posts within their first week.

## Works with

## When to use

When launching a new community, or when an existing community notices high join numbers but low actual participation - the welcome flow is often the highest-leverage fix for a "ghost town" feeling despite decent member count.
